skill.name: cicd-intelligent-recovery
skill.description: Automates CI/CD failure analysis, root cause detection, sandbox validation, and feedback reporting for development workflows. It coordinates GitHub Actions data, multi-agent analysis, repair planning, test validation, and pull request generation.
skill.summary: Automate CI/CD failure recovery with root cause analysis and validation workflows.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified extensive external command, filesystem, network, and environment access patterns, most of which are CI/CD automation examples rather than hidden malware. No prompt injection text or confirmed exfiltration payload was found, but the workflow asks agents to process untrusted CI data, upload changed file contents, create webhooks, push branches, and create pull requests. This is high risk for marketplace publication without stronger user approval, sanitization, and repository safety controls.

high_findings.title: Automated repository modification and publication
high_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run git add, commit, push, create a pull request, and update GitHub status after automated fixes. This is legitimate CI/CD behavior, but it can publish incorrect or unsafe code if an LLM-generated repair is wrong or influenced by untrusted inputs. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for high-risk automation. Confidence: 0.88. Confidence reasoning: Direct git and GitHub publication commands are present, and the surrounding workflow delegates repair and validation to automated agents.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct git and GitHub publication commands are present. The workflow delegates repair and validation to automated agents before publication.

high_findings.title: Untrusted CI data inserted into agent prompts
high_findings.description: Failure logs, error messages, and Loop 2 artifacts are embedded directly into Gemini and Task prompts. CI logs and repository data can contain adversarial text, so this creates a prompt-injection path into analysis and repair agents unless sanitized. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE semantic risk. Confidence: 0.84. Confidence reasoning: The workflow includes direct command substitution from parsed failure artifacts into LLM prompts without documented filtering or trust boundaries.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The prompt templates directly interpolate parsed failure data and artifacts. No sanitization or instruction-boundary handling is documented near these flows.

high_findings.title: Changed file contents uploaded to external sandbox service
high_findings.description: The workflow uploads every file returned by git diff HEAD --name-only to a claude-flow sandbox using the local file contents. This can disclose sensitive local changes if secrets, generated credentials, or private files are accidentally included in the diff. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for data exposure risk. Confidence: 0.76. Confidence reasoning: The upload loop is explicit, but the destination may be a legitimate sandbox service and the actual exposure depends on repository contents.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The upload loop sends local changed file contents to a sandbox command. The risk depends on what files are changed and how the sandbox service is configured.

medium_findings.title: GitHub webhook and API operations require broad repository trust
medium_findings.description: The skill creates or modifies GitHub repository hooks and statuses through gh api commands. This is expected for CI/CD recovery, but it changes repository integration state and should require explicit user approval. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE operational risk. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: The commands are explicit GitHub API operations, but they are aligned with the skill purpose rather than hidden behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The GitHub API operations are clearly documented. They are legitimate for CI/CD use but still modify repository infrastructure.

medium_findings.title: Extensive local artifact reads and writes
medium_findings.description: The workflow reads CI logs, writes parsed failure files, writes reports, and stores cross-loop memory artifacts. The filesystem access is expected, but the volume of file operations increases the chance of leaking or overwriting sensitive project data. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE moderate risk. Confidence: 0.74. Confidence reasoning: The file operations are explicit and mostly scoped to .claude artifacts and docs, but the workflow handles repository-derived data broadly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are explicit and mostly scoped. Risk remains because the skill handles CI logs, generated patches, and reports across the repository.

medium_findings.title: Environment variable comparison may reveal sensitive configuration
medium_findings.description: Troubleshooting guidance compares the local environment against a sandbox environment. Environment dumps can include credentials, tokens, and internal service names if copied into logs or prompts. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE moderate risk. Confidence: 0.72. Confidence reasoning: The command is present and environment output is sensitive by nature, although no hardcoded secret exfiltration was found.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment comparison command is explicit. It does not itself send secrets to an external endpoint, but output handling is not constrained.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography findings are terminology false positives
low_findings.description: Static findings for weak cryptographic algorithms map to generic words such as algorithm, graph algorithms, and connascence of algorithm. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, encryption, or password hashing implementation in the inspected context. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Confidence: 0.93. Confidence reasoning: The surrounding text describes software dependency analysis, not cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched terms are used for graph and software design analysis. No cryptographic implementation was found in the inspected locations.

low_findings.title: C2 keyword findings are workflow diagram false positives
low_findings.description: The graph file uses terms such as root cause, feedback, and process flow in a CI/CD diagram. No evidence found of command-and-control networking or malware coordination. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Confidence: 0.91. Confidence reasoning: The relevant file is a Graphviz process diagram for CI/CD recovery, not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged file is a process diagram. The inspected text describes root-cause workflow steps, not C2 behavior.

low_findings.title: Database connection strings are test placeholders
low_findings.description: The database and Redis URLs appear in a sandbox configuration example using test credentials and localhost endpoints. They are not real secrets, but examples should still warn users not to paste production credentials. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for secret leakage, with minor documentation risk. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The values are clearly local test placeholders, but the workflow handles environment variables elsewhere.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The connection strings use test credentials and localhost. The risk is documentation hygiene rather than confirmed credential exposure.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command substitution into LLM prompts
dangerous_patterns.description: Artifacts are inserted into LLM prompts through shell substitution. This can carry untrusted CI content into agent instructions unless the caller separates data from instructions. Confidence: 0.84. Confidence reasoning: Direct substitution is visible in prompt construction lines.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct shell substitution into prompt text is visible. No sanitization guidance is documented at those points.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad git publication commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow stages all repository changes, commits, pushes, and opens a pull request. This is powerful automation that needs explicit user review. Confidence: 0.88. Confidence reasoning: The command sequence is direct and affects remote repository state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The git and gh commands directly publish changes. This behavior is intentional but high impact.

dangerous_patterns.title: External sandbox upload of local file contents
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uploads every changed file content to a sandbox command. This can expose sensitive diffs if no allowlist is used. Confidence: 0.76. Confidence reasoning: The loop reads each changed file and passes its content to the upload command.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file upload pattern is explicit. Actual severity depends on sandbox trust and changed file contents.

content.user_title: Recover CI Failures with Intelligent Automation
content.value_statement: CI failures often hide root causes across logs, tests, and related files. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through analysis, repair planning, sandbox validation, and feedback reporting.
actual_capabilities: Guides collection and parsing of GitHub Actions failure logs.
Coordinates multi-agent analysis for root causes and failure cascades.
Plans fixes with connascence-aware impact analysis.
Runs sandbox validation steps for unit, integration, and end-to-end tests.
Generates differential reports, pull request text, and feedback artifacts.
limitations: Requires GitHub CLI authentication and project-specific CI access.
Assumes Node.js, jq, npx, and claude-flow commands are available.
Does not guarantee safe fixes without human review.
Can expose sensitive data if logs, diffs, or environment output contain secrets.

use_cases.title: Debug failing GitHub Actions runs
use_cases.description: Collect recent failures, parse logs, and identify likely root causes before repair work starts.

use_cases.title: Validate automated fixes before review
use_cases.description: Run repair candidates through sandbox tests and compare results against the original failures.

use_cases.title: Create evidence-rich CI feedback loops
use_cases.description: Package failure patterns, validation reports, and pull request context for future planning cycles.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize a failed CI run
prompt_templates.prompt: Use cicd-intelligent-recovery to review the latest failed GitHub Actions run. Summarize the failing tests, affected files, and first likely cause.

prompt_templates.title: Find root causes across failures
prompt_templates.prompt: Use cicd-intelligent-recovery to analyze parsed CI failures. Build a root cause map, separate primary failures from cascades, and list evidence for each claim.

prompt_templates.title: Plan safe repairs with validation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use cicd-intelligent-recovery to plan fixes for each confirmed root cause. Include affected files, expected side effects, required tests, and human approval checkpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Run the full recovery workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use cicd-intelligent-recovery for the full Loop 3 workflow. Collect CI data, analyze failures, prepare fixes, validate in sandbox, and draft a pull request summary for review.

output_examples.input: A GitHub Actions run fails after a dependency update.
output_examples.output: The skill produces a failure summary, likely dependency conflict, affected test groups, and validation steps for the proposed version change.

output_examples.input: Several tests fail after a shared authentication change.
output_examples.output: The skill separates the primary authentication issue from cascaded failures and recommends bundled fixes for shared callers.

output_examples.input: A repair branch needs review evidence.
output_examples.output: The skill drafts a pull request narrative with root causes, tests run, remaining risks, and links to generated artifacts.

best_practices: Review every generated command before it changes repository or GitHub state.
Sanitize CI logs and environment output before passing them into prompts.
Use a disposable branch and sandbox for all automated repair attempts.
anti_patterns: Do not run the full workflow against production credentials or private secrets.
Do not accept a 100 percent test claim without inspecting actual test output.
Do not let automated agents push changes without human review.

faq.question: Does this skill fix CI failures automatically?
faq.answer: It provides an automation workflow for analysis, repair planning, and validation. Human review is still required before publishing changes.

faq.question: Which CI system does it target?
faq.answer: The documented workflow targets GitHub Actions through the GitHub CLI and GitHub API commands.

faq.question: Can it work with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: What inputs does it need?
faq.answer: It expects a Loop 2 delivery package, parsed CI failure data, repository access, and authenticated GitHub CLI access.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The workflow processes untrusted CI data and can run commands that change repository and GitHub state.

faq.question: When should teams avoid this skill?
faq.answer: Avoid it when secrets are present in logs, approvals are not available, or the repository cannot tolerate automated repair attempts.
